# Amazon Carbon Credit Project Shows Striking Forest Protection — But a Volatile Middle Decade Raises Questions

**Satellite analysis of a Microsoft-backed Brazilian REDD project reveals genuine deforestation suppression overall, yet a single year of crisis saw tree loss surge to levels that dwarf the entire pre-project period**

The most striking number buried in the satellite record of the Cikel Brazilian Amazon REDD project isn't how much forest was saved — it's what happened in 2016. In that single year, 1,233.5 hectares of forest disappeared inside the project boundary, according to satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier. That one-year spike exceeds the total forest loss recorded across the entire eight years *before* the project began, which amounted to just 1,622 hectares between 2001 and 2008. It is a figure that demands scrutiny from any organisation that purchased credits generated during that period.

## The Satellite Timeline

The project, registered under Verra's Verified Carbon Standard as VCS832, covers 27,435 hectares of Brazilian Amazon and began operations on 1 April 2009. The Hansen Global Forest Change dataset, analyzed by CarbonVerifier using the project's registered polygon geometry, records forest cover at the year 2000 baseline as 205,961 hectares — a figure that immediately signals the registered area sits within a far larger forested landscape.

Pre-project losses were modest if uneven: 21.9 hectares lost in 2001, rising sharply to 546.3 hectares in 2005, falling back to 45.1 hectares in 2006, then climbing again through 264.5 hectares in 2007 and 489.8 hectares in 2008. The cumulative pre-project total stood at 1,622 hectares across eight years.

The project's opening years appeared to deliver. Loss in 2009 dropped to just 49.5 hectares, and 2012 recorded only 10.9 hectares — the lowest single-year figure in the entire 23-year dataset. Years 2011 through 2014 were all below 105 hectares, suggesting genuine suppression of clearing pressure.

Then 2015 arrived. Loss jumped to 386.5 hectares. In 2016 it exploded to 1,233.5 hectares — nearly three times the next-highest post-project year. Recovery followed: 517.9 hectares in 2017, declining through 413.3 in 2018, 391.5 in 2019, a low of 141.4 in 2020, before rising again to 484.2 hectares in 2021. The final two years on record, 2022 and 2023, show 258.9 and 167.4 hectares respectively.

Sentinel-2 NDVI imagery corroborates the Hansen data. The proportion of pixels classified as deforested fell from 2.1% in 2018 to 1.3% in 2020, then crept back to 2.2% by 2024, consistent with the pattern of partial recovery after the mid-decade crisis followed by renewed, if slower, degradation.

## The Additionality Question

The project's most important defence comes from its comparison against five nearby unprotected control areas at the same latitude. Satellite data analyzed by CarbonVerifier shows those controls lost between 8.44% and 37.28% of their 2000 forest cover over the same period, for a mean loss rate of 27.1%. The project, by contrast, lost 2.12% of its 2000 forest cover after the start date — a difference of nearly 25 percentage points.

CarbonVerifier's verdict is unambiguous: **STRONG additionality**. The forest inside VCS832 has, in aggregate, fared dramatically better than comparable unprotected land nearby. Control area 1 alone lost 73,731 hectares out of 197,754 — a 37.28% loss rate that makes the project's 2.12% look remarkable.

## What This Means for the Voluntary Carbon Market

Microsoft is listed as a known credit buyer for this project. The aggregate evidence gives that purchase more credibility than many REDD deals currently under scrutiny: the forest is genuinely still standing at rates far above the counterfactual. But the 2016 anomaly — 1,233.5 hectares gone in twelve months — exposes a fundamental weakness in how carbon credits are issued and retired. Credits may have been sold and counted against corporate emission targets during years when the project was haemorrhaging forest at historically unprecedented rates within its own boundary.

The voluntary carbon market has repeatedly promised that REDD projects represent permanent, additional, verified emission reductions. This dataset shows that even a project with a strong long-run additionality signal can experience catastrophic single-year failures. Without transparent, year-by-year credit issuance linked to year-by-year satellite verification — rather than smoothed multi-year accounting — buyers have no way of knowing whether the carbon they paid for was real in the year they claimed it.
